nutrient_source.title: Double fish soup (no milk) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Composition Database, CR ID F03020003
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient composition database for the exact record 'Double fish soup (no milk)', CR ID F03020003, provenance canonical, confidence 0.95. All core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate), sugar, sodium, and dietary fibre are taken verbatim from this approved primary source. Saturated fat (0.5g per 100g) is estimated from HPB data for comparable Singaporean fish-based soup dishes, as the HPB record for this item does not separately report saturated fat.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Double Fish Soup (No Milk) contains only 1.39g carbohydrate per 100g. A standard 400g serving delivers approximately 5.6g available carbohydrate — far below the 50g minimum required for GI testing per the standard methodology described in Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic index is therefore not applicable and is set to null. gi_category is set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 reflecting the negligible carbohydrate contribution. The peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables are cited as the methodological authority confirming that GI cannot be meaningfully assigned to this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 436mg per 100g; a standard 400g bowl delivers approximately 1744mg sodium, representing 76% of the 2300mg daily limit in a single serving.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid consuming the full soup broth, request no added soy sauce or salt, and limit portion size to reduce sodium load substantially below the per-100g figure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is only 1.39g per 100g and sugar is 0.79g per 100g; the high protein content (3.21g per 100g) from the double fish portion supports stable postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management as a low-carbohydrate, protein-rich option; patients should avoid adding sweet sauces and monitor any accompanying rice or noodle portions separately.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); however, sodium at 436mg per 100g poses a cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation, particularly in a full-bowl serving.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the omega-3 content of the fish but should limit broth consumption and request reduced-salt preparation to mitigate the high sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) reduce joint inflammation; however, sodium at 436mg per 100g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory omega-3 content of the fish but should request reduced broth and no added soy sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Fish Soup (No Milk) is a Singaporean hawker-style clear broth soup featuring a double portion of fish — typically a combination of fresh sliced fish and deep-fried sliced fish — served with vegetables in a light, savoury broth. The dish is a variant of the popular Singaporean fish soup (yu tou mi fen tang) but omits the evaporated milk that is sometimes added to create a richer, creamier broth. The result is a clean, delicate soup with a mild umami flavour derived from the fish and broth. The fresh fish slices are silky and tender, while the deep-fried fish pieces add a contrasting crispy-then-softened texture after soaking in the broth. Vegetables such as tomato, lettuce, or tofu may be included. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (30 kcal per 100g), low in carbohydrates (1.39g per 100g), and provides a meaningful protein contribution (3.21g per 100g) from the double fish portion. Fat content is moderate at 2.36g per 100g, with saturated fat estimated at approximately 0.5g per 100g. Sodium is elevated at 436mg per 100g, making broth management important for sodium-sensitive individuals. The dish is naturally dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal. It is commonly consumed as a main meal at Singaporean hawker centres and food courts, often served with rice or bee hoon (rice vermicelli) on the side. The fish provides omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular and joint health. This dish is particularly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate and sugar content.
